behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition
behaviors.justification: Git rename detection depends on calling git with appropriate flags (e.g., --find-renames) and correctly parsing git's rename representation. A pure unit mock would only test parsing of a specific canned output, but would miss failures where: (1) the tool doesn't call git with rename detection enabled, (2) the code incorrectly interprets git's rename output format, or (3) the rename similarity threshold is wrong. Only a real repository with actual git operations catches these boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test git repository, rename a file in a commit, run the tool on that commit, verify the output categorizes it as 'renamed' (not 'deleted' + 'added'). Test with various rename percentages (100% unchanged content, partial edits during rename).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing against real git behavior is necessary; mocking git output is insufficient.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output
behaviors.justification: Git represents binary files differently in its output (e.g., 'Binary files differ', no text diff available). A mock could return canned binary-file markers, but would miss: (1) how the tool handles actual git binary output format, (2) crashes from attempting to read binary content as text, (3) platform-specific binary handling issues, (4) the exact representation git uses for binary changes. Only testing against real git operations on actual binary files catches these failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository with a real binary file (e.g., PNG, PDF), commit it, modify it, run the tool, verify: (1) no crash, (2) binary file is listed in the report with correct category, (3) counts include the binary file.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real binary files and real git commands required to detect crashes and misparsing.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error
behaviors.justification: Git behaves distinctly in a repository with no commits (e.g., HEAD doesn't exist, git log returns nothing). A mock could return empty data, but would miss: (1) off-by-one errors in iteration logic, (2) null-pointer or missing-key crashes, (3) incorrect error handling for 'no commits' state. Only a real empty repository with actual git commands reveals these edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Create a fresh git repository with zero commits (git init only), run the tool, verify: (1) no error/crash, (2) output is a valid empty report structure.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Edge case behavior of git with no commits must be tested against real git state.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed
behaviors.justification: This is the core correctness requirement. A mock passing this test would be meaningless—the test only validates whether our code matches real git behavior. Unit tests with mocked git output could pass while the actual parsing is broken, off-by-one errors exist, or edge cases are missed. Must create known commits and verify tool output against ground truth from actual git log/diff.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository with a known sequence of changes: add N files, delete M files, rename K files, modify L files across multiple commits. Run the tool. Verify report counts exactly match: (1) git log output, (2) git diff --name-status output, (3) manual verification of each change category.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Verifying accuracy requires real data and real git operations; mocking would bypass the actual test.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged
behaviors.justification: Testing that staging state doesn't affect output requires real git staging area behavior. A mock could assume staging doesn't matter, but would miss: (1) code that accidentally reads working directory state when it should read committed state, (2) real git interactions with the index, (3) timing issues between staging and analysis. Only real git operations reveal whether the tool correctly isolates committed history from staging.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository with committed files. Make changes and stage some but not all. Run the tool and capture output. Unstage all changes. Run the tool again. Verify both outputs are identical (if tool analyzes committed history) or consistent in the expected way.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real git staging area needed to test isolation of analysis from working directory state.
